explicit_text: Tift Park was the site of a pivotal moment in my activism when we organized the 'pool jump' to integrate the pool there. Back then, it was a segregated space, and in the early 1960s, we planned a direct action to challenge that. After jumping into the pool with others, we caused such a stir that they drained and scrubbed it for days. Today, it’s the Boys' Club, and I even went back inside with Peter de Lissovoy to share our story with the new generation.
implicit_text: There was a pivotal moment in my activism when we organized the 'pool jump' to integrate the local community pool. Back then, it was a segregated space, and in the early 1960s, we planned a direct action to challenge that. After jumping into the pool with others, we caused such a stir that they drained and scrubbed it for days. Today, the building serves as the Boys' Club, and I even went back inside with a fellow activist to share our story with the new generation.

explicit_text: Joni Rabinowitz was right there with me at the mass meeting where the idea to jump in the Tift Park pool was born. I whispered the idea to her, and she immediately shouted it out to the group, making it official. She was a key part of that moment, helping spark the action that became a major demonstration for integration.
implicit_text: She was right there with me at the mass meeting where the idea to jump in the community pool was born. I whispered the idea to her, and she immediately shouted it out to the group, making it official. She was a key part of that moment, helping spark the action that became a major demonstration for integration.

explicit_text: We used the African Methodist Episcopal Church, or CME as we called it, as a gathering spot before the pool jump. It was affectionately nicknamed 'Crime, Murder, and the Electric chair' by some, but for us, it was a safe place to organize. We met on someone's back porch nearby to break into groups and plan our approach to the pool.
implicit_text: We used the local African Methodist Episcopal Church as a gathering spot before the pool jump. It was affectionately nicknamed 'Crime, Murder, and the Electric chair' by some, but for us, it was a safe place to organize. We met on someone's back porch nearby to break into groups and plan our approach to the pool.

explicit_text: Phil Davis was one of the leaders who helped organize the volunteers for the Tift Park pool jump. He had all the names of the seventy-five volunteers and led one of the groups that approached the pool from different directions. Phil’s role was crucial in coordinating the effort and making sure everyone knew their part.
implicit_text: One of the leaders who helped organize the volunteers for the pool jump had all the names of the seventy-five volunteers and led one of the groups that approached the pool from different directions. His role was crucial in coordinating the effort and making sure everyone knew their part.

explicit_text: Peter de Lissovoy was another key figure in the pool jump; he led one of the groups approaching the pool. Later on, Peter and I went back to Tift Park to tell the current staff about how we changed the scene with our actions. He also joined James Daniels in painting the black coachmen white around town, showing how we sometimes did things to irritate the establishment in those days.
implicit_text: Another key figure in the pool jump led one of the groups approaching the pool. Later on, he and I went back to the site to tell the current staff about how we changed the scene with our actions. He also joined another activist in painting the black coachmen white around town, showing how we sometimes did things to irritate the establishment in those days.

explicit_text: Jake Wallace was with me and James Daniels during the pool jump at Tift Park. When we couldn’t get around the back of the pool, we decided to abort but then took a chance and jumped the fence anyway. We swam across the pool and escaped through the back gate, eventually making it to Arcadia Church for safety.
implicit_text: He was with me and another activist during the pool jump. When we couldn’t get around the back of the pool, we decided to abort but then took a chance and jumped the fence anyway. We swam across the pool and escaped through the back gate, eventually making it to a nearby church for safety.

explicit_text: James Daniels was right there with Jake Wallace and me during the pool jump at Tift Park. He was part of the group that decided to jump the fence and swim across the pool despite the police presence. James also took part in painting the black coachmen white around town, and later we sneaked back to the pool to throw in dye as a prank.
implicit_text: He was right there with us during the pool jump. He was part of the group that decided to jump the fence and swim across the pool despite the police presence. He also took part in painting the black coachmen white around town, and later we sneaked back to the pool to throw in dye as a prank.

explicit_text: During the pool jump demonstration, there were women at the front gate singing 'We Shall Overcome' as they tried to buy tickets to the pool. Their singing was a powerful act of protest, and many of them went to jail for it. The song was a symbol of our movement and helped keep spirits strong during those tense moments.
implicit_text: During the pool jump demonstration, there were women at the front gate singing 'We Shall Overcome' as they tried to buy tickets to the pool. Their singing was a powerful act of protest, and many of them went to jail for it. The song was a symbol of our movement and helped keep spirits strong during those tense moments.

explicit_text: After we jumped in the pool at Tift Park, Jake, James, and I ran across Jefferson Street to escape. It was just a short distance to an alley and then to Arcadia Church where we found refuge. That street was part of our route away from the police that day.
implicit_text: After we jumped in the pool, we ran across a nearby street to escape. It was just a short distance to an alley and then to the church where we found refuge. That street was part of our route away from the police that day.

explicit_text: Arcadia Church was a sanctuary for us after the pool jump at Tift Park. We climbed a tree nearby dripping wet, and three deacons came out to check on us. They let us inside because they knew the police wouldn’t search the church, so it was a safe haven during that tense time.
implicit_text: That church was a sanctuary for us after the pool jump. We climbed a tree nearby dripping wet, and three deacons came out to check on us. They let us inside because they knew the police wouldn’t search the church, so it was a safe haven during that tense time.

explicit_text: SNCC was where I fully committed to the Movement after the pool jump incident. I went straight to the SNCC office after changing clothes, and James Daniels was already bragging about what we did. I even took the money I was supposed to use to buy a car and gave some of it to Joyce Barrett at SNCC for groceries, showing how central the organization was to our activism.
implicit_text: I fully committed to the Movement after the pool jump incident by going straight to the local civil rights committee office after changing clothes. One of my fellow activists was already bragging about what we did. I even took the money I was supposed to use to buy a car and gave some of it to a committee member for groceries, showing how central the organization was to our activism.

explicit_text: Earl Bronson owned the grocery store where I worked at Third and Washington Streets. After the pool jump, he called me out for knowing about 'them niggers' who jumped in the pool, which hit me hard and really committed me to the Movement. Later, when I tried to repay him for a loan to buy a car, he threatened to sue me, but I stood my ground and walked out with friends buying food to show him we weren’t backing down.
implicit_text: The grocery store owner where I worked at the corner of two streets called me out for knowing about the people who jumped in the pool, which hit me hard and really committed me to the Movement. Later, when I tried to repay him for a loan to buy a car, he threatened to sue me, but I stood my ground and walked out with friends buying food to show him we weren’t backing down.

explicit_text: Third Street was part of my daily life because I worked at Earl Bronson’s grocery store on the corner of Third and Washington. It was there after the pool jump that the racial slur from Earl Bronson hit me the hardest and pushed me deeper into the Movement. It was a place where the personal and political collided for me.
implicit_text: That street was part of my daily life because I worked at the grocery store on the corner of two streets. It was there after the pool jump that the racial slur from the store owner hit me the hardest and pushed me deeper into the Movement. It was a place where the personal and political collided for me.

explicit_text: Washington Street intersected with Third Street where I worked at the grocery store owned by Earl Bronson. After the pool jump, the confrontation with Earl happened right there, which was a turning point in my activism. That corner became a symbol of the struggle we faced daily.
implicit_text: The street intersected with another where I worked at the grocery store owned by the man who confronted me. After the pool jump, the confrontation happened right there, which was a turning point in my activism. That corner became a symbol of the struggle we faced daily.

explicit_text: Joyce Barrett was with SNCC and helped me when I brought some of the money I was supposed to use for a car loan. I believe she took some of it for groceries because we didn’t always have much good food at the SNCC house. She was part of the support network that kept us going during those tough times.
implicit_text: She was with the local civil rights committee and helped me when I brought some of the money I was supposed to use for a car loan. I believe she took some of it for groceries because we didn’t always have much good food at the committee house. She was part of the support network that kept us going during those tough times.

explicit_text: Giles was a grocery owner who helped SNCC by sending over canned goods without labels, which Sherrod peeled off so no one would eat the best food first. His contributions helped keep us fed when resources were scarce. Giles was one of the many people quietly supporting the Movement.
implicit_text: A grocery owner helped the civil rights committee by sending over canned goods without labels, which a fellow activist peeled off so no one would eat the best food first. His contributions helped keep us fed when resources were scarce. He was one of the many people quietly supporting the Movement.

explicit_text: Wendy Mann was one of the women I took with me back to Earl Bronson’s grocery store after the pool jump. We went there to buy food and show off a little after our demonstration. She was part of the group that stood with me during that tense encounter with Earl.
implicit_text: One of the women I took with me back to the grocery store after the pool jump went there to buy food and show off a little after our demonstration. She was part of the group that stood with me during that tense encounter with the store owner.

explicit_text: Penny Patch was another woman who went with me to the grocery store after the pool jump to buy food. We were making a statement by showing up there with money and buying supplies despite the tension. She was part of the solidarity we had in those moments.
implicit_text: Another woman went with me to the grocery store after the pool jump to buy food. We were making a statement by showing up there with money and buying supplies despite the tension. She was part of the solidarity we had in those moments.

explicit_text: I think Miriam Cohen was the third woman who went with Wendy Mann and Penny Patch to the grocery store after the pool jump. We were all together making a statement by buying food and standing up to Earl Bronson. She was part of that group that showed our strength and unity.
implicit_text: I think a third woman went with the other two to the grocery store after the pool jump. We were all together making a statement by buying food and standing up to the store owner. She was part of that group that showed our strength and unity.
